projects.name: Chromatic Mirror v1
projects.desc: Live colour mosaic and generative art sampled directly from your webcam. No tracking - pure pixel-level palette extraction.

projects.name: Chromatic Mirror v2
projects.desc: Live colour mosaic and generative art sampled directly from your webcam. No tracking - pure pixel-level palette extraction.

projects.name: Presence Theremin
projects.desc: Hand position controls pitch and volume of a Web Audio synth. An invisible instrument you play with empty air.

projects.name: Mirror Kaleidoscope
projects.desc: Your webcam feed reflected and rotated into a real-time symmetrical kaleidoscope. Adjust segments and mirror modes live.

projects.name: Gaussian Splat Viewer
projects.desc: Gaussian FlyThru is a browser-based Gaussian splat viewer for flying through 3D captures, adding bookmarks, hotspots, camera defaults, bounds, screenshots, recordings, and workspace exports.
projects.fullDesc:
Gaussian FlyThru is a browser-based Gaussian splat / Luma .ply viewer designed for inspecting, navigating, annotating, and recording immersive 3D captures.

It loads LUMA-style Gaussian splat .ply files directly in the browser, so a user can explore a captured real-world scene without needing a heavy desktop 3D package. The viewer supports multiple navigation modes: Orbit for inspecting the scene from outside, Walk for grounded movement, Look for mouse-based first-person viewing, and Flight Sim for flying through the capture with velocity control, mouse steering, a central reticle, and optional bounding-box containment.

The project includes tools for setting a camera movement boundary, aligning the bounding box across X/Y/Z rotations, adjusting splat size, showing an axis gizmo, saving camera defaults, and reading precise camera position and rotation data. Users can create editable viewpoint bookmarks, add notes, generate flythrough tours from saved viewpoints, scrub a timeline, set tour duration, adjust look-ahead and aim smoothing, and record tours as MP4/WebM where supported.

It also supports hotspots: labeled markers placed inside the 3D scene with editable text, position, color, and short labels. These are useful for identifying scene features, research observations, design issues, or spatial points of interest.

The app exports and imports workspace JSON files. These save viewer state such as scene alignment, bounds, camera defaults, current camera, bookmarks, tour settings, hotspots, splat size, axis visibility, screenshot/recording settings, and navigation settings. The actual .ply capture remains separate and must be loaded again before importing the workspace.

For immersive practice and research, Gaussian FlyThru could be used to study spatial experience, document captured environments, rehearse movement paths, create guided walkthroughs, compare viewpoints, annotate field observations, and generate video material from 3D scans. It is especially relevant for architecture, exhibition design, virtual production, site analysis, heritage documentation, urban research, performance spaces, training simulations, and any workflow where moving through a captured environment matters as much as viewing it.

projects.name: CaptureLab   Screenshot Record Paint
projects.desc: CaptureLab is a browser tool for capturing screens, recording video, sketching, layering, annotating, cropping, selecting, and exporting visual research material for immersive practice and documentation.
projects.fullDesc:
CaptureLab is a browser-based capture, recording, and visual annotation workspace designed for documenting screen-based activity and developing visual research material.
   
It has three modes. Screenshot mode lets the user select a screen or window, capture still images, store them in a temporary gallery, and edit each capture with its own layer stack. Record mode records screen video, with options for audio, quality, resolution, format, and saved output. Paint mode provides a blank or imported-image canvas for sketching, diagramming, annotation, and visual development.
   
The project includes drawing tools such as pen, eraser, fill, colour picker, text, crop, rectangular selection, lasso selection, and transform/select. Shape tools include rectangles, ellipses, lines, arrows, stars, speech bubbles, and thought bubbles, with editable line/fill styling, opacity, palettes, and layer controls. Layers can be named, hidden, reordered, merged, deleted, selected, moved, scaled, rotated, and edited after creation.
  
It imports screen captures and image files, and exports still images as PNG or JPG at different scales. Recording exports video formats such as MP4 or WebM, depending on browser support.
  
For immersive practice and research, CaptureLab can be used to document interaction flows, analyse software or virtual environments, annotate spatial/visual experiences, create research diaries, capture usability observations, sketch interface ideas, and produce visual evidence from screen-based practice. It is especially useful where screenshots, notes, mark-up, layered interpretation, and short recordings need to stay close together in one lightweight working space.

projects.name: Stop Motion Studio
projects.desc: A standalone browser-based stop-motion animation tool. Capture frames via webcam or import images, edit on a timeline, rotoscope, add an audio scratch track, and export as MP4, GIF, or image sequences.
projects.fullDesc:
Stop Motion Studio is a fully self-contained, browser-based animation tool built as a single HTML file requiring no installation, server, or internet connection. It runs directly from a local file, making it immediately deployable in any classroom, studio, or research environment with a browser.
    
At its core, the tool is a frame-by-frame animation system. A live webcam feed is displayed in one window while a preview window shows captured or imported frames. Frames can be captured directly from the camera or imported as image sequences, and are arranged on a zoomable, scrollable timeline with IN and OUT range markers for non-destructive editing. Frames can be duplicated, deleted, reordered, or extended in duration by dragging — and the playback rate is fully adjustable, supporting standard animation frame rates from 8 to 30fps.
    
The rotoscoping system allows artists to draw frame-by-frame annotations directly over the captured footage using a pen, eraser, or line tool with a 25-colour editable palette. These drawings exist on a separate transparent layer that can be toggled, exported independently as a PNG sequence, and composited with the captured frames on export. An onion skin feature overlays up to three previous frames as semi-transparent ghosts on the live camera, providing spatial reference for animators during capture.
     
An audio scratch track can be imported, scrubbed, and mixed into the final export. Output formats include MP4 with embedded audio, animated GIF, PNG and JPG sequences. Project settings — including all camera, timeline, guide, and rotoscope configurations — are saved and restored via a JSON project file.
       
The tool has strong potential for immersive practice and research. Its rotoscoping and compositing capabilities support investigations into presence, embodiment, and the boundary between live capture and drawn mark-making. The frame-extend and onion skin features enable durational studies — tracing movement, gesture, and spatial relationships across time. As a portable, dependency-free tool, it is equally suited to field-based research, participatory workshops, and classroom-based experimental animation practice.

projects.name: Panzerkrieg Editor
projects.desc: Panzer Editor is a browser-based 3D tool for designing, configuring and testing WWII tanks for the game Panzerkrieg. Build models, tune combat stats, and run target practice, all exported as a single GLB file.
projects.fullDesc:
Panzer Editor is a self-contained browser tool built for designing and configuring WWII tank assets for the game Panzerkrieg. It requires no installation, runs from a single HTML file, and stores everything - geometry, combat data, explosion physics - inside a standard GLB file using the GLTF extras field.
    
The editor is organised across three distinct modes. 
   
1. The Model tab is a full 3D mesh editor with orthographic views, transform controls, material editing, and a role-tagging system that tells the engine which parts of the tank are hull, turret, or barrel. 
   
2. The Armoury tab assembles the modelled tank into a live drive simulation where the user can physically operate the vehicle — steering, accelerating, firing shells — against a field of spawned enemy tanks on a 500×500 world-unit terrain. Three shell types (HE, AP, LR) are each independently configurable with their own ballistic properties, recoil behaviour, and fire rates. A terrain texture system with normal map support and a flight simulator-style Drone Camera round out the environmental tools. 
   
3. The Enemy Tank tab provides a dedicated editor for configuring explosion and shrapnel physics, which are then embedded in the enemy GLB and triggered automatically when a shell connects in the Armoury.
   
Beyond game development, the tool has meaningful potential for immersive military education and research. The ability to model historical vehicle configurations, set accurate performance parameters such as turret traverse speed, armour rating, and ammunition characteristics, and then experience those parameters interactively through a live drive simulation creates a uniquely tangible learning environment. Researchers could use it to explore comparative tank performance, while educators could deploy it as an accessible, no-install simulation tool.

projects.name: Asteroids
projects.desc: Asteroids is a browser-based 3D space combat simulatio. A single self-contained HTML file in which the player pilots a spacecraft to intercept and destroy an autonomous boid navigating a procedurally generated asteroid field.
projects.fullDesc:
Asteroid is a browser-based interactive 3D simulation built entirely in a single self-contained HTML file. It runs in Chrome without a build process, importing Three.js r158 and OrbitControls as ES modules from a CDN, with no external dependencies, servers, or frameworks required.
   
The simulation places the user inside a procedurally generated asteroid field — a field of rotating, tumbling cubes scattered across a defined volume — and tasks them with piloting a first-person spacecraft to intercept and destroy an autonomous target called the Boid. A second autonomous agent, Princess, chases the same target object independently, creating emergent triangular dynamics between the player, the quarry, and the observer. Both agents use quaternion-based steering with asteroid avoidance built from weighted repulsion vectors, giving them naturalistic flight behaviour without scripted paths.
   
The environment supports two camera modes: a first-person flight camera with inertial mouse controls and a full six-degree flight model, and an orbital observer camera that allows detached viewing of the scene. Space mines of two types — orange (non-fatal) and red (fatal) — add tactical hazard, with full collision detection, camera shake, and escalating warning sequences. Missile combat uses alternating twin-gun emitters, a lock-on detection cone, and a proximity-based HUD reticle. Death events trigger one of two cinematic destruction scenarios driven by real-time quaternion interpolation, particle systems, and procedural audio.
   
The project has direct application in immersive media research and education as a platform for exploring autonomous agent behaviour, spatial reasoning, multi-agent interaction, and real-time 3D interface design — all in a lightweight, portable format that runs in any modern browser without installation.

projects.name: Space Invaders: A Prompt Engineering Case Study
projects.desc: Effective prompting is used to generate a complete arcade game. However, it is explicitly cautioned that without design thinking, iteration, or analytical engagement, the process collapses into retrieval, not learning.
projects.fullDesc:
This project is a fully self-contained browser-based recreation of the 1978 Taito arcade classic Space Invaders, generated entirely from a single structured prompt without external dependencies, frameworks, or asset files. The game runs directly in any modern browser as a standalone HTML document, rendering all visuals programmatically through the Canvas 2D API using pixel-by-pixel bitmap drawing routines. It imports and exports nothing (no libraries, no network calls, no build pipeline) making it genuinely portable and archivable.

The game was created in less than 5 mins with 2 prompts.
     
Prompt 1 : "Please describe to me the game "SPACE INVADERS". Outline in detail how the gameplay unfolds, how the controls are used, and how the graphics are created."
   
Prompt 2 : "Now write an extensive prompt to create this game."
      
Functionally, the game replicates the original with considerable fidelity: three alien types march in formation with accelerating speed as their numbers diminish, firing projectiles that physically erode destructible shields pixel by pixel. A mystery UFO traverses the screen periodically, awarding variable bonus scores. The player cannon responds to keyboard and touch input, with a single-shot constraint that demands timing and precision. All audio is synthesised in real-time through the Web Audio API, including the iconic four-tone march sequence, noise-burst kill effects, and frequency-sweep death sounds. A persistent high-score is stored locally between sessions.
     
In the context of immersive media practice and research, this project carries specific pedagogical and methodological relevance. The technique of generating interactive, real-time browser environments through structured natural language prompting has direct applications in rapid prototyping for installation work, interactive narrative, and experiential design research. A researcher could deploy this methodology to produce testable interactive prototypes for audience studies, spatial computing experiments, or immersive environment pre-visualisation, collapsing weeks of development into hours. 
     
This project is offered as a demonstration of prompt engineering capability, not an advocated model of practice. Generating a functional application through a single prompt, while technically impressive, bypasses the iterative reasoning, critical problem-solving, and developmental thinking that constitute genuine learning, reducing the process to sophisticated retrieval rather than constructed understanding. What would begin to legitimise this work is the subsequent stage: the deliberate interrogation, adaptation, and modification of the generated output, where generated material becomes the starting point for critical authorship rather than the endpoint of a query. Students and practitioners are strongly cautioned against treating generative output as a substitute for technical and conceptual competency; its responsible use presupposes the very analytical capacity it is otherwise capable of circumventing.

projects.name: GLB File Viewer / Slideshow
projects.desc: GLB Slideshow is a self-contained browser-based 3D viewer built with Three.js. It supports loading, viewing and presenting GLB files with full lighting, camera, metadata and scene-state controls — all saved as a curated, reloadable slideshow playlist.
projects.fullDesc:
GLB Slideshow is a self-contained, browser-based 3D presentation and research tool built entirely in a single HTML file using Three.js r160. It requires no installation, no server, and no build pipeline — opening it in any modern browser gives immediate access to a full-featured GLB viewing and curation environment.
       
The tool operates in two complementary modes. In standard viewing mode it functions as a professional GLB viewer with granular control over lighting (exposure, ambient, key, fill and rim lights with studio/outdoor/dramatic presets), camera (FOV, orbit, auto-rotate with bidirectional speed control), background (greyscale and custom colour swatches, background image with brightness and opacity blending), grid, display and overlay settings. All of these scene attributes can be embedded directly into a GLB file as metadata alongside authoring information — name, author, contact, tags, description and notes — and recalled precisely on reload.
   
In slideshow mode a horizontal filmstrip panel opens along the bottom of the viewport, accepting GLB files by drag and drop. Each card in the filmstrip stores its own complete scene state — camera position and orientation, lighting values, background colour, grid settings, rotation speed, and metadata overlay style — which is restored automatically when the card is selected. Slideshows are saved as lightweight JSON playlists and reloaded by pointing to a folder of GLB files, making the format portable and archival.
   
Export options include PNG screenshots, transparent-background PNG renders for compositing, and GLB files with embedded scene state and version history.
     
For immersive practice and research the tool has significant potential as a presentation layer for 3D asset libraries, a comparative review environment for iterative model development, a lightweight alternative to game-engine previsualisaton pipelines, and a portable portfolio or exhibition format that travels as two files — a JSON playlist and a folder of GLBs — with no dependency on cloud infrastructure or proprietary software.

projects.name: Flight Simulator
projects.desc: Flight Simulator — A browser-based 3D flight simulator built as a single HTML file using Three.js. Features a fully functional flight model, HUD overlays, cockpit skin, instrumentation panels, and realistic thrust/speed/pitch mechanics.
projects.fullDesc:
This is a browser-based 3D flight simulator built as a single self-contained HTML file, requiring no installation or build tools. It runs entirely in Chrome using Three.js r128 for 3D rendering and HTML5 Canvas for HUD and instrument overlays.
        
The simulator presents a first-person cockpit perspective over a procedurally populated 3D environment of coloured cubes and stars, with a fully functional flight model governing thrust, speed, pitch, roll, yaw, altitude, and landing gear. The aircraft starts on the ground with brakes applied, requiring the pilot to build thrust, release brakes, and manage a realistic runway roll before achieving takeoff speed. Once airborne, pitch controls climb and descent, roll and yaw respond proportionally to speed, and a graduated pitch ease system simulates the inertia of a real aircraft transitioning from ground to flight.
      
The interface has two configurable side panels — a Setup Panel controlling environment, lighting, atmosphere, scene objects, and a cockpit skin overlay — and a Flight Control Panel showing live instrumentation including IAS, altitude, VSI, heading, and fuel. A heads-up display canvas renders a heading tape, speed and altitude ladders with thrust markers, a boresight reticle, and a pitch ladder that rotates with the aircraft's roll angle. A separate cockpit instrument panel canvas aligns precisely with a loadable PNG cockpit skin at 1920×1080.
      
The simulator can import GLB/GLTF 3D models and custom cockpit skin PNGs, making it extensible for different vehicle configurations. Its potential applications include immersive UX research for cockpit interface design, spatial awareness training, HUD readability studies, and as a development platform for interactive aerial experiences and educational simulations.

projects.name: Boids  v1
projects.desc: Boids 01 is a Three.js 3D simulation where a single agent (Boid) seeks and follows a bouncing target within a bounded space, using velocity and turn-rate limited steering to create realistic, gradual movement.
projects.fullDesc:
Boids 01 is a real-time 3D steering and flocking simulation built as a self-contained browser application using Three.js. At its core, a single triangular agent navigates a bounded volumetric space by seeking a moving target object, using quaternion-based steering constrained to local X and Y rotation axes, producing smooth, lifelike turning behaviour governed by adjustable velocity and turn-rate parameters.
   
The environment is fully interactive — the camera can be orbited, zoomed and panned freely, and all key simulation parameters are exposed through a structured control panel. The bounded space itself is a resizable wireframe box whose height can be adjusted at runtime, while the target object bounces dynamically off all six interior faces, creating an unpredictable flight path for the agent to pursue. Visual aids including local axis gizmos, directional light helpers, a shadow-casting ground plane and a world-space orientation gizmo help communicate spatial relationships clearly during observation.
   
The application imports Three.js via CDN as an ES module alongside OrbitControls and GLTFLoader, and exports scenes as binary GLB files, making it straightforward to extract simulation geometry into external 3D tools for further development.
   
In the context of immersive practice and research, Boids serves as a lightweight, accessible testbed for exploring emergent agent behaviour, spatial reasoning and non-linear choreography. It has strong potential as a tool for visualising autonomous movement systems in virtual and extended reality contexts, generative installation work, or as a pedagogical instrument for teaching steering algorithms and spatial computing. The architecture is intentionally modular, designed to scale toward full multi-agent Reynolds flocking with separation, alignment and cohesion forces.

projects.name: Boids  v2
projects.desc: A real-time 3D flocking simulation built with Three.js. Multiple agents fly together using Reynolds' three rules — separation, alignment, and cohesion — producing emergent flock behaviour controlled via an interactive panel.
projects.fullDesc:
Boids is a real-time 3D flocking simulation built as a self-contained browser application using Three.js. It implements Craig Reynolds' classic emergent behaviour algorithm, spawning a flock of agents that fly together through a bounded 3D space without any central coordination — cohesion, separation, and alignment forces applied locally to each agent every frame produce the complex group dynamics observed in real bird murmurations and fish shoals.
  
The simulation offers two broad operational modes: a contained mode where soft wall repulsion and hard boundary clamping keep the flock within a defined box, and an open mode where the flock drifts freely through unbounded space. A secondary attractor — a bouncing target sphere — can be introduced to draw the flock in a particular direction, overlaying goal-seeking behaviour on top of the emergent dynamics. An auto-rotating camera keeps the flock continuously in frame, and all flocking parameters — agent count, speed range, turn rate, perception radius, and the individual weights of each Reynolds force — are exposed as real-time sliders, allowing the behaviour to be sculpted live without reloading.
  
The application imports Three.js via CDN ES module along with OrbitControls, GLTFLoader, and GLTFExporter, and can export the full scene as a GLB file for use in external 3D tools or game engines.
  
For immersive practice and research, Boids serves as a versatile generative engine. It can drive ambient environmental behaviour in virtual reality installations, model crowd or swarm dynamics for spatial computing experiments, provide a visual thinking tool for studying emergence and complexity, and act as a real-time generative backdrop for audiovisual performance work.

projects.name: Boids  v3 - X-Wing
projects.desc: A real-time 3D flocking simulation built with Three.js, implementing Craig Reynolds' Boids algorithm. A flock of tetrahedra exhibit emergent flocking behaviour — cohesion, separation, alignment — while navigating cylindrical obstacles and floor avoidance within a bounded space.
projects.fullDesc:
X-Wing_Boids is a self-contained, browser-based 3D flocking simulation built entirely with Three.js (r158). It implements Craig Reynolds' classic Boids algorithm — cohesion, separation, and alignment — to produce emergent collective behaviour from a squadron of agents navigating a bounded 60×60 unit space in real time at 50fps.
    
The simulation runs in two model modes: a custom flat-shaded tetrahedron mesh, or a loaded X-Wing GLB model embedded directly in the file. Agents respond to a bouncing target object, six positionable cylindrical obstacles with configurable detection radii and avoidance weighting, and a floor avoidance zone that pitches agents upward using local-axis rotation. Obstacle avoidance uses a predictive look-ahead system, projecting each agent's future position to trigger evasion before collision occurs. A full set of sliders governs every aspect of flock behaviour — speed range, turn rate, perception radius, and the relative weights of each steering force.
    
The camera system supports orbital navigation, auto-rotation, and a Track Flock mode that dynamically centres the orbit target on the average position of the leading agents. The scene includes dynamic lighting with shadow mapping, a customisable grid with colour and intensity controls, and adjustable background tone.
     
The file imports Three.js, OrbitControls, GLTFLoader, and TransformControls exclusively via CDN — no build tools, no server required. It exports nothing, running entirely in the browser as a single HTML file.
     
In the context of immersive practice and research, the simulation serves as a real-time behavioural testbed for exploring emergence, swarm intelligence, and spatial dynamics — directly applicable to crowd simulation, generative installation design, interactive narrative environments, and the choreography of autonomous agents in virtual production and XR contexts.

projects.name: Motion Trail Painter v1
projects.desc: See Motion Trail Painter 01

projects.name: Motion Trail Painter v2
projects.desc: See Motion Trail Painter 02

projects.name: Image Mesh Morph
projects.desc: MeshMorph is a self-contained browser-based image morphing tool. Users place correspondence nodes on two images to guide a mesh warp, exporting a smooth H.264 MP4 transition video. Runs locally from file:// with zero dependencies.
projects.fullDesc:
MeshMorph is a self-contained, browser-based image morphing tool that runs entirely from a local HTML file with no server, installation, or internet connection required. The user loads two images — a start frame and an end frame — then places numbered correspondence nodes across both images to define how specific points in one image relate to points in the other. These nodes guide a mesh-based warp algorithm that smoothly transitions the pixel content of one image into the other over a user-defined duration of one to ten seconds.
     
The tool offers three warping methods — Delaunay triangulation, Thin Plate Spline, and Moving Least Squares — each producing subtly different deformation qualities ranging from sharp, localised warps to globally smooth, organic transitions. Five easing curves control the temporal feel of the morph, from linear to bounce. Users can prepend a customisable title card, freeze the first or last frame for dramatic effect, and optionally burn the animated mesh overlay directly into the exported video. Everything is exported as a standards-compliant H.264 MP4 file encoded entirely in the browser via the WebCodecs API.
    
Sessions — including all node positions, method choices, easing, duration, and title settings — can be saved and reloaded as lightweight JSON files, making iterative refinement practical.
    
For immersive practice and research, MeshMorph offers a precise, tactile way to explore transformation, identity, and perceptual continuity between images. It could be used to morph portraits, architectural spaces, anatomical forms, or environmental states — producing material for projection mapping, VR content pipelines, performance documentation, and live visual research where the act of morphing itself carries conceptual weight.

projects.name: GLB Editor
projects.desc: A self-contained, single-file browser-based 3D editor built on Three.js. Supports loading, creating, transforming, grouping and exporting GLB scenes. Designed as a reusable template component for projects requiring interactive 3D object editing.
projects.fullDesc:
GLB Editor is a fully self-contained, browser-based 3D scene editor built on Three.js r160, packaged as a single HTML file with no build tools or server requirements. It runs entirely in the browser, making it immediately deployable across platforms without installation.
      
The editor loads GLB and GLTF files via drag-and-drop or file picker, normalises them into a navigable 3D viewport with orbit, zoom and pan controls. Once loaded, scenes can be decomposed — grouped objects can be exploded into individually selectable meshes, each inheriting their correct world-space transforms. Objects can then be selected individually or as multi-object selections, transformed (translate in world space, rotate and scale in local space) via on-screen gizmos or precise numeric inputs, duplicated, grouped, deleted, or have their normals reversed. All structural changes support five-level undo. New geometry can be created directly in the editor — boxes, spheres, cones and cylinders — with configurable parameters, choice of material type (Wireframe, Basic, Lambert, Phong, Standard), and editable colour. Transforms can be baked into geometry, aligning local axes to world axes. The scene exports as a single GLB file.
      
For immersive practice and research, the editor functions as a lightweight spatial prototyping tool — enabling rapid construction, modification and export of 3D scenes without dependency on commercial software like Blender or Maya. It is particularly suited to preparing assets for WebXR experiences, AR/VR pipelines, and real-time rendering contexts where GLB is the standard delivery format. Its single-file architecture makes it embeddable as a template component within larger research platforms, curatorial tools, or interactive documentary and spatial storytelling projects.

projects.name: Generic 3D Environment Setup
projects.desc: A web-based 3D environment with camera and light controls. Built on Three.js r160, featuring orbit/zoom/pan, FOV, auto-rotate, custom grid, directional lighting with shadows, and an XYZ gizmo.
projects.fullDesc:
3D Project is a web-based, self-contained 3D environment built on Three.js r160, delivered as a single HTML file with no build step or external dependencies beyond a CDN. It opens directly in any modern browser, making it immediately accessible for research, prototyping, and presentation contexts.
          
The environment provides a fully interactive perspective viewport with orbit, zoom, and pan controls driven by mouse input, alongside a structured dark control panel. Camera behaviour is tunable in real time — field of view, damping, and auto-rotation speed can all be adjusted without reloading. A custom grid system built from a single LineSegments object provides spatial reference without the rendering artefacts associated with Three.js's native GridHelper.
         
Lighting is composed of an ambient layer, a key directional light, and a fill directional light, each independently controlled for intensity and visibility. PCF soft shadow mapping is enabled on the key light, with a wide frustum configured to cover the full scene. Tone mapping and exposure are adjustable through the panel, giving broad control over the perceived look of any content loaded into the scene.
     
The file imports Three.js and OrbitControls as ES modules via CDN, and exports nothing — it is entirely self-contained. The scene is intentionally left empty beyond the ground plane and grid, making it a clean drop-in foundation for any 3D content.
        
For immersive practice and research, this template is well suited as a starting point for spatial composition tools, object placement studies, lighting investigations, virtual exhibition prototypes, real-time material and shader explorations, and browser-based 3D documentation of physical or speculative work.

projects.name: Start & End Frame Extractor
projects.desc: A frame extraction tool for chaining AI-generated video scenes — save the first and last frames of any clip to use as start/end reference frames when generating adjacent scenes.
projects.fullDesc:
Frame Extractor is a lightweight, self-contained browser tool designed to bridge the gaps between AI-generated video scenes. It operates entirely locally — no installation, no server, no cloud dependency — running as a single HTML file in any modern browser.
       
The workflow is straightforward: load any MP4, MOV, or WebM video file via drag-and-drop or file browser, and the tool automatically seeks to and captures both the very first and very last frame of the clip at its native resolution. Both frames are previewed side by side with their exact pixel dimensions and timestamps displayed. Once captured, frames can be exported individually or together in PNG (lossless), JPG, WebP, or BMP format, with adjustable quality control for lossy formats.
       
The core value lies in what those two frames enable. Contemporary AI video generation tools — Runway, Kling, Sora, and others — allow users to specify a starting or ending reference image to anchor a generated scene. By extracting the last frame of one clip and feeding it as the first-frame prompt of the next, and vice versa, practitioners can stitch together coherent, continuous AI-generated sequences without visual discontinuity between scenes. This makes Frame Extractor an essential utility for constructing longer-form immersive narratives, generative film work, virtual environment walkthroughs, and experiential installations where spatial or temporal continuity across scenes is critical to the viewer's sense of presence.
    
Crucially, it removes the need to open heavyweight production software like Adobe Premiere or After Effects simply to grab a single frame — a task that previously required importing footage, scrubbing to position, and manually exporting a still.

projects.name: Pixel Dust
projects.desc: A browser-based tool that selects, animates, and exports pixel-level image differences as H.264 MP4 video. A key proof-of-concept demonstrating direct pixel manipulation and pure-JS MP4 encoding via the WebCodecs API.
projects.fullDesc:
Pixel Dust is a browser-based creative tool for animating the pixel-level differences between two images and exporting the result as a true H.264 MP4 video file — entirely without a server, plugins, or external libraries.
The tool begins by loading two images: a clean background plate and a version of the same scene containing an additional object. It performs a per-pixel colour difference analysis, which can be refined using a paintbrush directly on the diff mask canvas, allowing precise manual inclusion or exclusion of pixels. A resolution slider consolidates pixels into blocks of up to 5×5, giving the animation a mosaic or chunky particle quality.
      
Two animation modes drive the export. In Dissolve Right, selected pixels drift horizontally with velocities derived from their individual brightness values — brighter pixels travel faster, darker ones slower. In Explode mode the system becomes substantially richer: each pixel is assigned a directional velocity vector drawn from four weighted axes, modified by gravity, dampening, mass (brightness-linked weight), and a per-pixel random factor. Pixels are given individual lifespans — again brightness-derived — and fade out through alpha compositing. A Fuse Wire system allows the user to place a Start and End handle on the canvas, creating a radial wave of staggered launch times that propagates outward from the start point. A brightness delay layer adds a further perceptual stagger within that wave. The entire animation can be rendered in reverse.
        
The tool exports genuine H.264 MP4 via the WebCodecs API with a pure-JavaScript muxer — no FFmpeg, no server.
   
For immersive practice and research, Pixel Dust opens direct investigation into how images decompose, how matter appears to dissolve or shatter, and how light-weight (brightness-as-physics) can be used as a compositional principle. It has clear application in real-time installation work, generative video performance, and research into perception of material transformation.

site.title: Immersive Lab
site.subtitle: The projects here exist at the intersection of exploration and imagination. A collection of real-time creative experiments using webcam, MediaPipe, and the Web Audio API, built through vibe coding: an intuitive, improvisational approach to making where curiosity leads and outcomes follow. They exist to probe, to test, to ask what if. Because imagination needs space before it needs purpose - and the most interesting discoveries tend to live just past the point where the plan ran out.
site.about:
This sandbox is a exploratory environment where interactive and immersive technologies can be tested, broken, and reassembled without consequence. Built through vibe coding: an intuitive, improvisational approach to making where curiosity leads and outcomes follow.

Many of the projects here have no immediate purpose beyond the experience of discovering what happens when technology is given room to play. They exist to probe, to test, to ask what if. Because imagination needs space before it needs purpose - and the most interesting discoveries tend to live just past the point where the plan ran out.

Potential rarely arrives fully formed. It surfaces through experimentation - through the willingness to begin without knowing the end.

- Chris Shaw
Head, Puttnam School of Film & Animation
LASALLE College of the Arts
University of the Arts Singapore
